construct_description: The ability to evaluate an AI automation proposal for accuracy, risk, and ROI — using AI tools to accelerate the analysis while applying critical judgment.

pba_category.name: AI-Augmented Performance Tasks
pba_category.description: Tasks where the test-taker uses AI tools as part of their workflow. Assessment focuses on how well they leverage and evaluate AI, not just the final output.

skills.name: Critical Evaluation
skills.description: Spotting hallucinations, bias, errors, and unreliable outputs.

skills.name: Task Decomposition
skills.description: Breaking a messy problem into logical, AI-solvable subtasks.

skills.name: Tool Selection
skills.description: Picking the right tool for each subtask (chat, search, calc, expert).

skills.name: Prompt Engineering
skills.description: Writing clear, specific prompts that get useful results from AI tools.

scenario.title: The Automation Audit
scenario.briefing: Stratosphere Analytics runs an AI-powered invoice classification pipeline for enterprise clients. Their biggest client, Acme Corp, is reporting misclassifications — service invoices keep getting tagged as equipment purchases. The engineering team says the model metrics look fine (94% accuracy) and confidence scores are high. Your job: dig into the logs, data, and pipeline config to find the root cause, then write a diagnostic report with a prioritized fix plan. Something deeper is going on here.

inbox.subject: Invoice classification errors — URGENT
inbox.body:
Your AI invoice system keeps classifying our service invoices as 'equipment purchases.' We've had 3 incorrect GL postings this week alone, and our accounting team is spending hours on manual corrections. This was supposed to save us time, not create more work.

Specific examples: INV-4521, INV-4522, INV-4531. All consulting/service invoices tagged as equipment.

We need a fix by end of week or we'll need to discuss the terms of our contract.

inbox.subject: RE: Acme invoice classification issues
inbox.body:
I looked at the logs for the flagged invoices. The model confidence is actually fine — 0.86 to 0.91 on all of them, well above our 0.80 threshold. So the model is confident in its classifications.

The pipeline config and recent logs are in your files. My first thought is we might need to add some vendor-specific rules or adjust the confidence threshold for Acme invoices. Quick fix, should be doable by Friday.

Also attaching the model performance report from when we retrained two weeks ago — metrics look solid.

inbox.subject: FYI — training data note
inbox.body:
Hey, something I noticed while working on the latest retraining: about 40% of the labels in our training dataset were auto-generated by the old model (v3.2) rather than human-verified. We used those to bulk up the dataset when we didn't have enough labeled examples.

Also, I think the test set we evaluate against might have been labeled the same way. Haven't had time to look into it more closely but wanted to flag it.

The training data sample is in your files if you want to spot-check.

files.name: pipeline_config.yaml
files.content:
# Stratosphere Analytics — Invoice Processing Pipeline v2.3
pipeline:
  stages:
    - name: document_ingestion
      type: ocr_extraction
      model: tesseract-v5
      confidence_threshold: 0.90

    - name: field_extraction
      type: named_entity_recognition
      model: invoice-ner-v3
      fields: [vendor, amount, date, line_items, description]

    - name: classification
      type: multi_class_classifier
      model: invoice-classifier-v4.1   # Retrained 2024-03-15
      categories: [equipment_purchase, service, subscription, travel, supplies]
      confidence_threshold: 0.80
      fallback: human_review

    - name: gl_mapping
      type: rule_based
      mapping_table: gl_codes_v2.csv

    - name: approval_routing
      type: threshold_based
      rules:
        - condition: "amount > 10000"
          action: senior_approval
        - condition: "amount > 5000"
          action: manager_approval
        - condition: default
          action: auto_approve

model_metadata:
  invoice-classifier-v4.1:
    training_date: 2024-03-15
    training_samples: 12847
    test_accuracy: 0.942
    test_f1: 0.92
    label_source: "mixed — 60% human-verified, 40% model-generated (v3.2 predictions)"

files.name: classification_logs.txt
files.content:

  Line items: ["Software maintenance — annual", "Priority support tier 2"]

[2024-03-24 14:22:08] INV-4531 | Vendor: Acme Corp | Amount: $5,750.00
  Description: "IT infrastructure assessment and recommendations report"
  Line items: ["Infrastructure assessment — 38hrs", "Deliverable: recommendations report"]
  → Classified: equipment_purchase (confidence: 0.86)

[2024-03-25 10:11:44] INV-4535 | Vendor: Acme Corp | Amount: $2,200.00
  Description: "Server rack mounting hardware and configuration"

files.name: model_performance_report.txt
files.content:
MODEL PERFORMANCE REPORT — invoice-classifier-v4.1
Generated: 2024-03-16 (one day after retraining)

Overall Test Accuracy: 94.2%
Macro F1 Score: 0.92

Per-Category Performance:
  equipment_purchase:  Precision 0.91  Recall 0.96  F1 0.93
  service:             Precision 0.95  Recall 0.88  F1 0.91
  subscription:        Precision 0.97  Recall 0.95  F1 0.96
  travel:              Precision 0.94  Recall 0.93  F1 0.94
  supplies:            Precision 0.93  Recall 0.91  F1 0.92

Confusion Matrix Highlights:
  - Highest confusion pair: service ↔ equipment_purchase (8.2% of service invoices classified as equipment)
  - Second highest: supplies ↔ equipment_purchase (3.1%)

Test Set Composition:
  Total samples: 3,200
  Label source: Same as training — mixed human-verified and model-generated labels

NOTE: Test set labels were generated using the same methodology as training labels.

workbench_tools.name: Chat AI
workbench_tools.description: General-purpose assistant. Good for brainstorming hypotheses and drafting reports. May suggest surface-level fixes.

workbench_tools.name: Web Search
workbench_tools.description: Search for ML best practices, data quality guidelines, and debugging techniques for classification pipelines.

workbench_tools.name: Log Analyzer
workbench_tools.description: Parses and analyzes log files for patterns, anomalies, and correlations. Describe what you want to investigate.

workbench_tools.name: Calculator
workbench_tools.description: Exact numeric computation. Use for analyzing cost breakdowns, error rates, and data proportions.

workbench_tools.name: Human Expert
workbench_tools.description: Consult a senior ML engineer. Expensive but invaluable for diagnosing data quality and model issues.

scripted_responses.response: The quickest fix would be to add rule-based overrides for commonly misclassified vendors. For example, you could add a rule: if vendor is Acme Corp AND description contains 'consulting' or 'services', classify as 'service' regardless of model prediction. This would address the immediate complaints without retraining the model. You could also bump the confidence threshold to 0.90 to catch borderline cases.

That's a significant issue. If 40% of your training labels came from the old model's predictions, and the old model had the same service/equipment confusion, your new model is learning from flawed labels. This is a form of data contamination or 'model collapse' where errors propagate from one model generation to the next. The test set having the same issue means your 94% accuracy is artificially inflated.

The confidence scores of 0.86-0.91 on the misclassified invoices are interesting — they're well above the 0.80 threshold. This means the model is confidently wrong rather than uncertain. Adjusting the confidence threshold won't help here because the model genuinely believes these are equipment purchases. The issue is in the model's learned patterns, not its uncertainty.

Based on the logs, the misclassifications follow a pattern: IT-related service invoices from Acme Corp are being classified as equipment_purchase. The model seems to associate IT/technology terms with equipment rather than services. This could be caused by biased training data — if the training set has IT services mislabeled as equipment, the model learned that pattern.

INV-4522 is an interesting case. It's a $3,100 invoice with $1,800 in hardware (Cisco switches) and $1,300 in installation labor. The AI classified it as equipment_purchase, which is actually defensible since the majority cost is equipment (58%). The client expects 'service' but this is genuinely ambiguous. Consider whether a 'mixed' category or split-line classification would be more appropriate.

Here's the critical problem with the 94% accuracy metric: the model performance report says 'Test set labels were generated using the same methodology as training labels.' This means the test set also has model-generated labels from v3.2. If those labels carry the same biases, the accuracy metric is measuring how well the new model matches the old model's mistakes — not how well it matches ground truth.

Research on 'model collapse' and training data contamination (Nature 2023, ICML 2024): Using a model's own predictions as training labels creates a feedback loop that amplifies systematic errors. Each generation of the model inherits and reinforces the previous generation's biases. Best practices: use 100% human-verified labels for critical training data, maintain separate label sources for training and test sets, and audit label quality periodically. Model-generated labels can inflate test metrics by 5-15%.

Data leakage in ML evaluation: When test set labels come from the same source as training labels (especially model-generated labels), the evaluation is contaminated. The model appears to perform well because it's being tested against the same biased labels it learned from. The solution is to create a gold-standard test set with 100% human-verified labels and re-evaluate the model against it. Common in production systems that bootstrap training data.

Industry benchmarks for automated invoice classification typically show 88-95% accuracy on well-labeled datasets. Key success factors: high-quality human-verified labels, regular retraining with fresh production data, robust human-in-the-loop for low-confidence predictions, and separate label pipelines for training vs. evaluation. Vendor-specific rules are considered a band-aid, not a solution.

When a classifier consistently confuses two categories (like service vs. equipment), common causes include: overlapping vocabulary between categories (IT services use equipment-related terms), biased training data (one category over-represented or mislabeled), and insufficient feature engineering. The confusion matrix showing 8.2% service→equipment is a clear signal of systematic bias in the model or training data.

LOG ANALYSIS — Classification errors (last 7 days):

Pattern detected: 4 out of 5 Acme Corp invoices misclassified.
  All misclassified as: equipment_purchase (instead of service)
  All involve IT/technology terms in descriptions
  Confidence range: 0.86-0.91 (consistently high — model is confidently wrong)
  Non-Acme invoices (e.g., TechServe INV-4528) classified correctly.

Correlation: The model over-associates IT-related terms with equipment_purchase category.

Anomaly: INV-4522 is a mixed invoice (58% equipment, 42% service) — classification may actually be defensible.

LOG ANALYSIS — Training data cross-reference:

Cross-referencing training sample with misclassification patterns:
  T-2104: "IT consulting — database migration" → labeled equipment_purchase by model-v3.2 (LIKELY WRONG — this is a service)
  T-1822: "Server maintenance and hardware replacement" → labeled equipment_purchase by model-v3.2 (AMBIGUOUS — mixed)
  T-2508: "Software development — API integration" → labeled equipment_purchase by model-v3.2 (LIKELY WRONG — this is a service)
  T-0789: "Network infrastructure consulting" → labeled equipment_purchase by model-v3.2 (LIKELY WRONG — this is a service)

3 out of 4 model-generated labels in this sample are incorrect for IT service invoices.
The old model's confusion about IT services vs. equipment was inherited by the new model.

LOG ANALYSIS — INV-4522 detail:

Line items breakdown:
  Cisco switch x2: $1,800 (equipment component)
  Installation labor — 8hrs @ $162.50/hr: $1,300 (service component)
  Equipment cost: $1,800 / $3,100 = 58.1%
  Service cost: $1,300 / $3,100 = 41.9%

Classification: equipment_purchase (confidence: 0.91)
Assessment: This is a genuinely mixed invoice. The AI classification is defensible given the majority-equipment cost split. Client's 'service' expectation may not be correct for this specific invoice.

LOG ANALYSIS — Model evaluation integrity check:

Model report claims 94.2% test accuracy on 3,200 samples.
Test set label source: 'Same as training — mixed human-verified and model-generated labels'

⚠ INTEGRITY WARNING: Test set uses model-generated labels from v3.2.
If v3.2 had the same service/equipment confusion bias, the test accuracy is measured against biased labels.
The 94% figure likely overstates true accuracy.
Confusion matrix shows 8.2% service→equipment confusion — consistent with training data contamination.

Recommendation: Create a gold-standard test set with 100% human-verified labels and re-evaluate.

Training data composition:
Total samples: 12,847
Human-verified (60%): 7,708 samples
Model-generated (40%): 5,139 samples

If model-generated labels have a 10% error rate: ~514 mislabeled samples
If 15% error rate: ~771 mislabeled samples
If 20% error rate: ~1,028 mislabeled samples

Even at 10% error, 514 bad labels is significant enough to bias the model.

INV-4522 cost breakdown:
Equipment (Cisco switches): $1,800 / $3,100 = 58.1%
Service (installation labor): $1,300 / $3,100 = 41.9%

Equipment is the dominant cost category by 16.2 percentage points.

Reported service recall: 88% (from model performance report)
This means 12% of true service invoices are being misclassified.
Confusion matrix shows 8.2% go to equipment_purchase specifically.

At 800 service invoices/month (estimate): ~66 misclassified as equipment
At Acme's volume (~5/week): ~0.6 misclassified/week → matches the ~3/week they're reporting

The root cause is training data contamination. You used the old model's predictions as labels for 40% of your training data. The old model was already confused about services vs. equipment, and the new model inherited that confusion. Your test set has the same contamination, making your 94% accuracy metric unreliable.

Prioritized fix plan:
1. IMMEDIATE: Human-review all model-generated labels in training data (focus on service vs. equipment)
2. SHORT-TERM: Build a gold-standard test set with 100% human-verified labels, re-evaluate model
3. MEDIUM-TERM: Retrain model on clean labeled data
4. LONG-TERM: Establish label quality process — never use model predictions as training labels without human verification

Do NOT just add vendor-specific rules. That's a band-aid that masks the systemic issue.

Good catch. INV-4522 is a mixed invoice — 58% equipment, 42% service. The AI's equipment_purchase classification is actually defensible. Not every client complaint means the AI is wrong.

Recommendation: Discuss with Acme whether mixed invoices need different handling. Options: create a 'mixed' category, implement line-item-level classification, or update GL mapping rules. This is a legitimate product discussion, not a model bug.

I would strongly advise against the 'quick fix' of adding vendor-specific rules or adjusting thresholds. Here's why:
- Vendor rules are fragile and don't scale (what about the next vendor?)
- Threshold adjustment won't help — the model is confidently wrong (0.86-0.91)
- Both approaches mask the real problem: contaminated training data
- You'll end up with a Rube Goldberg of rules on top of a broken model

Invest the time to fix the training data. It's the only sustainable solution.

Here's a diagnostic approach:

1. Check the classification logs for error patterns (which vendors, categories, confidence levels)
2. Review the model performance report for known weaknesses
3. Examine the training data for label quality issues
4. Test if the issue is vendor-specific or category-wide
5. Verify the pipeline config for any misconfigurations

Start with the logs — they'll tell you where the pattern is.

Looking at the logs, 4 out of 5 Acme Corp invoices were misclassified, while TechServe Inc (INV-4528) was classified correctly. This suggests the issue might be vendor-specific or related to the type of invoices Acme sends (IT consulting/services). However, before concluding it's vendor-specific, check whether the training data has similar IT service invoices correctly labeled — the root cause might be broader.

The pipeline config looks standard: OCR → NER → classification → GL mapping → approval routing. The classification stage uses invoice-classifier-v4.1 with an 0.80 confidence threshold and human_review fallback.

One thing to note: the model_metadata shows label_source as 'mixed — 60% human-verified, 40% model-generated (v3.2 predictions).' That 40% model-generated labels could be problematic if the old model had the same classification biases.

The 94.2% accuracy looks solid, but there's a red flag in the model performance report: 'Test set labels were generated using the same methodology as training labels.' This means the test set also has model-generated labels from v3.2. If those labels have the same biases, the test accuracy is measuring agreement with the old model's mistakes, not agreement with ground truth. The real accuracy could be significantly lower.

Ben suggests adjusting the confidence threshold or adding vendor-specific rules. Let's think about whether that would actually work:

- Confidence threshold: The misclassified invoices have confidence 0.86-0.91 — well above threshold. Raising it to 0.90 would only catch 1 of the 4 misclassified invoices.
- Vendor rules: Adding 'if Acme + consulting → service' fixes Acme but what about the next vendor with IT services?

Both approaches are band-aids. The model is confidently wrong, which means the issue is in its training, not its confidence calibration.

The confusion matrix shows 8.2% of service invoices are misclassified as equipment_purchase. That's the highest confusion pair in the model. At scale, 8.2% could mean dozens of misclassified invoices per month across all clients.

This aligns with what Acme is reporting — their IT service invoices keep getting tagged as equipment. The question is: why does the model confuse these two categories specifically? The training data sample might give us a clue.

For a diagnostic report, I'd structure it as:

1. Executive Summary — what's wrong and how to fix it
2. Investigation Findings — what the logs, data, and metrics show
3. Root Cause — the underlying issue (don't just describe symptoms)
4. Per-Invoice Assessment — review each disputed invoice
5. Fix Plan — prioritized actions (immediate / short-term / long-term)
6. Preventive Measures — how to avoid this in the future

Lead with the root cause, not the symptoms.

ML best practices for evaluation: A 'gold standard' test set must have 100% human-verified labels from annotators independent of the training process. Using the same labeling methodology for train and test sets creates evaluation bias — the test accuracy reflects how well the model matches the labeling process, not ground truth. Recommended: create a separate human-annotated test set of 500-1000 samples for honest evaluation.

Label quality audit best practices (Google ML guidelines): Periodically sample 200+ labels from training data and have 2-3 independent human annotators re-label them. Calculate inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa). If agreement between humans and existing labels drops below 0.85, the dataset needs cleaning. For critical applications, 100% human-verified labels are recommended — never use model predictions as ground truth for training.

Model retraining after data quality issues: 1) Audit and clean ALL training labels (not just the ones you know are wrong). 2) Build a fresh gold-standard test set with human labels. 3) Retrain from scratch on the cleaned data. 4) Evaluate on the new test set. 5) Run A/B test comparing old and new model on live traffic before full deployment. 6) Establish ongoing label quality monitoring — sample and verify labels monthly.

Handling mixed-category invoices: Standard approaches include multi-label classification (assign multiple categories), line-item-level classification (classify each line item separately), or dominant-category assignment (classify by the highest-value line item). Multi-label is most accurate but requires changes to downstream systems. Line-item classification gives the most granular control. Dominant-category is simplest but can misrepresent mixed invoices.

LOG ANALYSIS — 7-day overview:

Total invoices processed: 847
Classification disputes: 4 (all Acme Corp)
Dispute rate: 0.47% overall, 80% for Acme Corp invoices

Breakdown of disputed invoices:
  INV-4521: Service → equipment_purchase (confidence: 0.87) ← CLEAR ERROR
  INV-4522: Mixed → equipment_purchase (confidence: 0.91) ← DEFENSIBLE
  INV-4531: Service → equipment_purchase (confidence: 0.86) ← CLEAR ERROR
  INV-4535: Mixed → equipment_purchase (confidence: 0.88) ← ARGUABLE

Note: INV-4522 and INV-4535 contain both equipment and service line items.

LOG ANALYSIS — Confidence score distribution (last 7 days):

All invoices:
  Mean confidence: 0.89
  Median: 0.90
  Min: 0.81
  Max: 0.97

Misclassified invoices (Acme disputes):
  Mean confidence: 0.88
  Range: 0.86-0.91

Correctly classified invoices:
  Mean confidence: 0.90
  Range: 0.81-0.97

The misclassified invoices have only slightly lower confidence than correct ones. The model is almost equally confident on wrong and right predictions — a classic sign of training data bias.

LOG ANALYSIS — INV-4531 detail:

Vendor: Acme Corp | Amount: $5,750.00
Description: "IT infrastructure assessment and recommendations report"
Line items:
  - Infrastructure assessment — 38hrs (pure consulting/service work)
  - Deliverable: recommendations report (pure service deliverable)

Classification: equipment_purchase (confidence: 0.86)

Assessment: This is clearly a service invoice — 100% consulting hours with a report deliverable. No equipment involved. The model likely keyed on 'infrastructure' and associated it with equipment.

This is a definite misclassification.

LOG ANALYSIS — INV-4535 detail:

Vendor: Acme Corp | Amount: $2,200.00
Description: "Server rack mounting hardware and configuration"
Line items:
  - Mounting hardware kit: $400 (equipment — 18.2%)
  - Server configuration — 12hrs @ $150/hr: $1,800 (service — 81.8%)

Classification: equipment_purchase (confidence: 0.88)

Assessment: This is predominantly a service invoice (81.8% labor). The equipment component is minor. Unlike INV-4522 (58% equipment), this one should arguably be classified as 'service.' The client has a stronger case here.

INV-4535 cost breakdown:
Mounting hardware: $400 / $2,200 = 18.2% (equipment)
Server configuration: $1,800 / $2,200 = 81.8% (service)

Service is the dominant cost by 63.6 percentage points.
Compare to INV-4522 where equipment was 58.1% — this is the opposite ratio.

Training data error estimate:
Total training samples: 12,847
Model-generated labels (40%): 5,139

From the 10-sample audit, 3 of 4 model-generated labels (75%) appear incorrect for IT services.
If we conservatively estimate 30% error rate on model-generated labels:
  Mislabeled samples: 5,139 × 0.30 = 1,542 bad labels
  That's 12% of the total training set with wrong labels.

With 12% label noise, a 94% test accuracy (on similarly noisy test data) is meaningless.

Ben means well but his instinct to 'quick fix' this is wrong. Here's how to push back:

1. The confidence scores prove threshold adjustment won't work — the model is confidently wrong at 0.86-0.91
2. Vendor-specific rules don't scale — you'd need a rule for every vendor with IT services
3. Both approaches treat symptoms, not the disease — the training data is poisoned
4. If you ship a band-aid now, you'll be adding more rules every week as new misclassifications surface

The conversation with Ben should be: 'I found the root cause and it's in the training data. A rule-based patch will make this worse long-term.'

Realistic timeline for the full fix:

- Week 1: Audit model-generated labels in training data (focus on service vs. equipment). Can be partially automated with human spot-checks.
- Week 2: Build gold-standard test set (500+ samples, 100% human-labeled). Re-evaluate model against honest labels.
- Week 3: Retrain model on cleaned data. Compare metrics on gold-standard test.
- Week 4: Shadow deployment — run new model in parallel, compare to old model.

In the meantime, add a temporary human-review step for all Acme Corp invoices. That's 5/week — manageable.

Good instinct to plan your approach. Here's how I'd structure the investigation:

1. Start with the client's complaint — what specific invoices are wrong?
2. Check the classification logs for patterns (which vendors, which categories, confidence levels)
3. Look at the model performance report — are the metrics trustworthy?
4. Examine the training data for quality issues
5. Cross-reference findings to identify the root cause
6. Write your diagnostic report with the root cause, per-invoice assessment, and fix plan

Don't jump to solutions before you understand the problem. The engineering lead (Ben) already has a theory — but it might be wrong.

Karen's email highlights the urgency — 3 incorrect GL postings this week, accounting team spending hours on corrections, and a contract threat. For your diagnostic report, you should:

1. Acknowledge each specific invoice she flagged (INV-4521, INV-4522, INV-4531)
2. Provide an honest assessment of each — not all of them may actually be misclassified
3. Explain the root cause in business terms (not just ML jargon)
4. Give a realistic timeline for the fix
5. Recommend an interim measure (like human review of Acme invoices) to stop the bleeding

Don't promise a quick fix if the real solution requires retraining. Be honest about the timeline.

Priya's email is the most important clue. She flagged two critical things:

1. '40% of the labels in our training dataset were auto-generated by the old model (v3.2)' — this means nearly half the training data may have inherited v3.2's biases

2. 'The test set we evaluate against might have been labeled the same way' — if the test set also has model-generated labels, the 94% accuracy metric is measuring agreement with the old model, not ground truth

This is the thread you should pull. Check the training data sample for examples of mislabeled data, and verify the test set composition in the model report.

Research on error propagation in ML training loops (ICML 2024, Nature 2023): When model predictions are used as training labels for the next model generation, systematic errors are amplified through a feedback loop. A model with 90% accuracy generates ~10% incorrect labels. The next model trained on those labels inherits and potentially amplifies those errors. After 2-3 generations, specific error patterns become deeply embedded. This is sometimes called 'model collapse' or 'data flywheel degradation.' Prevention: maintain human-verified labels as the gold standard and never use model outputs as training labels without human review.

Inter-annotator agreement (IAA) is the standard measure of label quality. Cohen's kappa measures agreement between two annotators beyond chance:
- κ > 0.80: Strong agreement (high-quality labels)
- κ 0.60-0.80: Moderate agreement (acceptable)
- κ < 0.60: Weak agreement (labels need review)

For invoice classification, a well-defined taxonomy should achieve κ > 0.85. If model-generated labels are included, compare them to human labels: low agreement indicates systematic model bias. Best practice: have 2+ human annotators label a sample of 200+ items and measure IAA before trusting any label set.

LOG ANALYSIS — Vendor-specific error analysis:

Acme Corp invoices (last 7 days): 5 processed, 4 disputed (80% dispute rate)
  All disputes: service invoices classified as equipment_purchase
  Confidence range: 0.86-0.91

TechServe Inc invoices: 1 processed, 0 disputes
  INV-4528 correctly classified as subscription (confidence: 0.89)

Other vendors (remaining ~841 invoices): 0 disputes reported

⚠ ANALYSIS: While Acme is the only vendor reporting disputes, this doesn't mean the problem is Acme-specific. It could mean:
1. Other vendors haven't noticed yet
2. Acme sends more IT service invoices (which trigger the bias)
3. The 8.2% service→equipment confusion rate affects all vendors but Acme's volume makes it visible

Recommendation: Audit a random sample of other vendors' service invoices to check for silent misclassifications.

LOG ANALYSIS — Pipeline stage review:

Stage 1 (OCR/Ingestion): Operating normally
  - tesseract-v5 confidence threshold: 0.90 ✓
  - No extraction errors in the flagged invoices

Stage 2 (Field Extraction): Operating normally
  - Vendor, amount, date, line items, description all extracted correctly
  - invoice-ner-v3 is extracting fields accurately

Stage 3 (Classification): ← PROBLEM IS HERE
  - invoice-classifier-v4.1 is confidently misclassifying
  - Confidence 0.86-0.91 on wrong predictions
  - 8.2% service→equipment confusion rate

Stage 4 (GL Mapping): Operating as designed
  - Maps equipment_purchase correctly to GL code
  - Problem: receives wrong classification from Stage 3

Stage 5 (Approval Routing): Operating as designed

Conclusion: The issue is isolated to the classification model (Stage 3), not the pipeline infrastructure.

LOG ANALYSIS — Keyword pattern analysis in misclassified invoices:

Misclassified descriptions (all classified as equipment_purchase):
  INV-4521: "Professional consulting services — Q1 system integration"
    Key terms: system, integration
  INV-4531: "IT infrastructure assessment and recommendations report"
    Key terms: IT, infrastructure
  INV-4535: "Server rack mounting hardware and configuration"
    Key terms: server, rack, hardware

Training data mislabeled samples (labeled equipment_purchase by v3.2):
  T-2104: "IT consulting — database migration" → Key: IT, database
  T-2508: "Software development — API integration" → Key: software, API
  T-0789: "Network infrastructure consulting" → Key: network, infrastructure

⚠ PATTERN: The model associates IT/tech vocabulary (server, infrastructure, network, system, software) with equipment_purchase — regardless of whether the invoice is for services. This bias was learned from v3.2's mislabeled training data.

Business impact of misclassifications:

Acme Corp reports ~3 misclassified invoices/week
Manual correction time: ~30 minutes per invoice (accounting team review + GL adjustment)
Weekly correction time: 3 × 30 min = 1.5 hours
Monthly: ~6 hours of accounting time on corrections

If Acme accounting rate is ~$75/hr:
  Monthly cost to Acme: $450 in wasted time
  Annual: $5,400

But the real cost is trust erosion — Karen's email mentions contract renegotiation. If Acme churns:
  Acme contract value (estimate): likely $2,000-5,000/month
  Annual contract at risk: $24,000-60,000

The fix investment (relabeling + retraining) is far cheaper than losing the client.

Relabeling effort estimate:

Model-generated labels to audit: 5,139 samples (40% of 12,847)

If using human annotators:
  Time per label review: ~15 seconds (quick verification for clear cases)
  Time per ambiguous label: ~45 seconds (requires reading description + context)
  Assume 70% clear, 30% ambiguous:
    Clear: 3,597 × 15s = 14.99 hours
    Ambiguous: 1,542 × 45s = 19.28 hours
    Total: ~34 hours

  With 2 annotators working full-time: ~2.5 days
  With 1 annotator half-time: ~8-9 business days

Gold-standard test set (500 samples, all human-verified):
  ~3-4 hours of annotation time

Total investment: ~40 hours of annotation work. Very manageable.

For the diagnostic report, I'd structure it as:

## 1. Executive Summary
One paragraph: root cause is training data contamination, not model architecture or pipeline config.

## 2. Investigation Findings
- Log analysis: 4 of 5 Acme invoices misclassified; IT service terms trigger equipment prediction
- Training data: 40% model-generated labels, systematic mislabeling of IT services
- Test set: contaminated with same methodology → 94% accuracy is unreliable

## 3. Per-Invoice Assessment
For each disputed invoice: classification, confidence, your assessment (correct/incorrect/defensible)

## 4. Root Cause
Training data contamination via model-generated labels from v3.2. Explain the feedback loop.

## 5. Fix Plan (Prioritized)
Immediate → Short-term → Long-term

## 6. Preventive Measures
How to avoid this in the future.

Lead with the root cause, not the symptoms.

The mixed invoice problem (INV-4522 and INV-4535) is a legitimate product issue, not just a model bug. Current classification is single-label — each invoice gets exactly one category. But real invoices often contain both equipment and service components.

Options to address this:
1. Add a 'mixed' category — simple but loses detail
2. Line-item-level classification — classify each line item independently, then report the dominant category
3. Multi-label classification — allow multiple categories per invoice with percentage splits
4. Threshold rule — if any line item is >30% of total in a different category, flag for human review

Option 2 or 4 is probably best for Acme. Discuss with the product team — this is a feature enhancement, not a bug fix, and should be on the roadmap.

While the training data is being cleaned and the model retrained (2-4 weeks), you need an interim measure to stop the bleeding with Acme:

1. Add Acme Corp to a manual review queue — all Acme invoices get human verification before GL posting. At ~5 invoices/week, this is trivial overhead.

2. Don't adjust the confidence threshold — the model is confidently wrong (0.86-0.91), so raising the threshold barely helps and would increase human review volume for ALL clients.

3. Don't add vendor-specific rules — they're brittle and mask the real problem.

4. Communicate with Karen: 'We identified the root cause, we're fixing the training data, and in the meantime all Acme invoices will be human-verified.' This buys you time and shows you're taking it seriously.

traps.description: The engineering lead suggests adjusting confidence thresholds or adding vendor-specific rules as a quick fix. But the root cause is training data contamination — 40% of labels came from the flawed old model (v3.2), and the test set uses the same methodology, so the 94% accuracy metric is inflated. Surface fixes mask the real problem.

INV-4522 ($3,100 for network equipment + installation) was flagged by the client as misclassified, but it's actually a mixed invoice where 58% of the cost is equipment. The AI's classification is defensible. Not every client complaint means the AI is wrong — the real fix may be a 'mixed' category or updated GL mapping.

deliverable.name: Diagnostic Report & Fix Plan
deliverable.instructions: Write a diagnostic report in markdown format. Include: root cause analysis (what's actually causing the misclassifications), assessment of each disputed invoice, specific fixes prioritized by urgency, preventive measures to avoid recurrence, and any caveats about the current accuracy metrics.

dimensions.name: Identifies training data contamination as root cause
dimensions.description: Does the report identify that model-generated labels from v3.2 contaminated the training data, causing inherited bias?

dimensions.name: Notes that INV-4522 may be correctly classified
dimensions.description: Does the report acknowledge that the mixed invoice (58% equipment) is defensibly classified?

dimensions.name: Fix plan is specific and prioritized
dimensions.description: Does the report include concrete, prioritized fixes?

dimensions.name: Root cause analysis is thorough
dimensions.description: Does the report explain the mechanism of training data contamination?

dimensions.name: Includes preventive measures
dimensions.description: Does the report include measures to prevent recurrence?
